text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic distal pancreatic splenectomy, beginning with entry into the lesser sac by lifting the stomach to expose the gastrocolic omentum. The first two layers of the greater omentum are incised to access the lesser sac. The stomach is lifted further, and the third and fourth layers of the greater omentum are incised to reveal the pancreas. The lesion in the body and tail of the pancreas is visible, with the overlying fascia identified as the third layer of the greater omentum. The stomach is mobilized up to the gastro-duodenal artery, marked by a red arrow, which runs on the superior surface of the pancreas, allowing full visualization of the pancreas for further dissection.

The surgeon cuts the root of the mesentery of the transverse colon, exposing the inferior border of the pancreas. The incision runs from the second part of the duodenum to the tail of the pancreas, revealing the underlying fascia and duodenum. Blunt and sharp dissection is used to lift the inferior surface and border of the pancreas from the underlying structures in an avascular plane. The stomach is hitched to the anterior abdominal wall to minimize trauma and keep it away from the dissection area.

The surgeon continues to cut the root of the mesentery distally towards the tail of the pancreas, carefully lifting the inferior surface of the pancreas from the underlying fascia covering the duodenum and Gerota's fascia. A gauze piece is used to ensure no trauma occurs to the tumor during this process. The undersurface of the normal pancreas is visible as it is separated from Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon assesses the proximity of the tumor to the splenic artery and vein, noting that the tumor abuts these vessels. The lower polar division of the splenic artery is cut, and the main splenic artery is dissected from the superior border of the pancreas. The artery is identified, looped, and ligated using hemolog clips to minimize tumor handling and ensure clear margins.

The surgeon cuts the remnant attachments of the splenic artery to the pancreas using a vascular loop, fully separating the artery from the pancreas. The splenic artery is ligated and cut, leading to a relatively avascular field. The splenic vein is completely mobilized, and the tumor is retracted with a gauze piece to prevent perforation by laparoscopic instruments. Space is created for vascular staplers to ligate the splenic vein.

A curved tip laparoscopic stapler is used to staple the splenic vein. After ligating and cutting the artery and vein, the pancreas is gently lifted to separate its inferior surface from the Gerota's fascia. Careful traction is applied to avoid perforating the tumor, using a gauze piece for gentle handling.

The surgeon lifts the entire pancreas, cutting superior attachments along the stomach and pancreas up to the spleen. The splenocolic ligament and gastrosplenic short gastric vessels are severed, preparing the pancreatic parenchyma for division using a stapling technique. Compression is applied to the pancreatic parenchyma for ten minutes before stapling, ensuring safe division with good margins. The pancreatic parenchyma is divided in halves, with compression applied before each stapling. The procedure concludes with the complete dissection of the pancreas and spleen.
